foreign_trope: All Americans are loud and hyper-positive
why_false_or_incomplete: Volume/enthusiasm varies strongly by region and setting
real_local_signal: friendly directness, explicit next steps, local/regional identity
use_instead: Use clear direct tone, not constant hype

why_false_or_incomplete: US is regionally huge; local norms vary
real_local_signal: state/city identity, sports, school, weather, commute, county/state systems

why_false_or_incomplete: Common in some workplaces, mocked in many others
use_instead: Use fewer idioms; one business phrase max if needed

why_false_or_incomplete: Class-loaded media trope; rural life varies by region
real_local_signal: county roads, trucks, schools, churches/civic groups, local work
use_instead: Use specific rural details without contempt

why_false_or_incomplete: Education and class norms vary widely
real_local_signal: grade level, campus, state school/private college/community college distinctions

why_false_or_incomplete: Small talk and service warmth can be sincere, ritual, or regional; directness varies
real_local_signal: brief greeting, clear ask, thanks, practical next step
use_instead: Use warmth without forced exclamation points

why_false_or_incomplete: Rural speech differs across New England, Midwest, Appalachia, Plains, West, Alaska, Hawaiʻi
real_local_signal: county/state terms, weather, school, land/work context
use_instead: Choose a region before rural markers

foreign_trope: American English is only General American media speech
why_false_or_incomplete: Local vowels and vocabulary persist, but many people style-shift
use_instead: Use light lexical signals rather than phonetic eye-dialect

foreign_trope: Every suburb is an HOA cul-de-sac
why_false_or_incomplete: Suburbs range from streetcar suburbs to immigrant gateway suburbs to exurbs
real_local_signal: schools, commute, apartments, strip malls, parks, local chains
use_instead: Name the suburb type instead of mocking suburbia
